summary: DHH describes his enthusiasm for freeware and shareware as an alternative form of tech rebellion, arguing you don't need to run Linux to feel like a countercultural force in computing. He identifies three benefits: the explorer's thrill of discovering new tools, the satisfaction of supporting individual developers rather than faceless corporations, and the feeling of fighting against software industry incumbents. This early post reveals the anti-corporate, indie-developer ethos that would later define much of his career.
key_points: Freeware and shareware offer a form of tech rebellion without requiring a full OS switch to Linux
The search for independent software tools is intrinsically rewarding, combining discovery with practical utility
Paying individual developers feels fundamentally different from paying corporations due to reciprocity and personal connection
Each independent software installation represents a small victory against dominant software incumbents
money_quotes: I don't have to give up Windows to get that self-gratifying rush of rebel disobedience.
From time to time I even get to relive how Columbus must have felt discovering America while looking for India.
I get to give something back to a developer that helped me -- A person with a name and a face.
I don't think I need to explain why that feels so much better than giving money to some faceless corporation.
Every installation is a new fortress and a victory, and I got one itch less to show for it.
I get to feel like I'm part of the rebels fighting The Empire.
key_insight: Supporting independent software developers is both a practical way to get better tools and an act of rebellion against corporate software dominance.
